bmrVsRmr.section1Intro: describe the body's energy expenditure at rest — how much energy your body burns when you are not moving at all.
bmrVsRmr.section1Box: BMR & RMR = Energy expenditure at rest
bmrVsRmr.section2Intro: is the energy expenditure in absolute rest. It is the body's "lowest possible" energy requirement to keep you alive.
bmrVsRmr.section3Intro: is the energy expenditure in realistic rest. You are awake, relaxed and still — but the body still needs energy to maintain posture, temperature and baseline muscle tension.
bmrVsRmr.section3Box: RMR is usually 5–10% higher than BMR
bmrVsRmr.section3UsedIn: RMR is most commonly used in:

ffmi.section1Intro: is a measure that describes the amount of fat-free mass (muscles, skeleton, organs) relative to your height. It gives a fairer picture of muscle mass than weight alone, because it accounts for body height.
ffmi.section3Item1: Track muscle gains independent of body fat
ffmi.section3Item2: Compare muscle mass between people of different heights
ffmi.section4Text: FFMI is affected by the accuracy of the body fat measurement. An inaccurate body fat measurement gives an inaccurate FFMI value.

lbmVsffm.section1Intro: and FFM both describe the body's "non-fat mass", but they are not identical.
lbmVsffm.lbmIntro: Consists of everything in the body except stored body fat, but includes:
lbmVsffm.practicalText1: Most methods (DEXA, calipers, bioimpedance) estimate FFM, not LBM.
lbmVsffm.practicalText2: That is why many formulas — e.g. Cunningham — use FFM.

maxFatMetabolism.section1P1: Alpert (2005) found that adipose tissue can deliver energy to fat-free mass up to approximately 290 kJ/kg fat/day (≈ 69 kcal/kg/day) in real experiments, and that the theoretical maximum flow is 358 kJ/kg fat/day (≈ 86 kcal/kg/day) if all FFM is protected. This value therefore represents the maximum energy flow from adipose tissue, not direct fat loss.
maxFatMetabolism.section1P2: In practical exercise and diet calculations, some sources want to convert Alpert's value into a daily "safety deficit" for fat loss. For this, a conservative fraction of 69 kcal/kg/day is often used, around 40–45%, to compensate for the fact that the body cannot utilise all energy directly due to basal metabolism, the thermic effect of food, and inefficiencies in oxidation. This gives approximately 31 kcal/kg fat/day, which is often used as a practical ceiling for daily fat loss in popular summaries.
maxFatMetabolism.section1P3: This simplified approximation appears in, among others, Lyle McDonald's popular science texts, such as The Rapid Fat Loss Handbook and articles on his blog, as well as in "Burn the Fat Blog" by Tom Venuto, where the value is presented as a guideline for how much fat can realistically be mobilised per day. It is important to note that this is not a direct empirical measurement from Alpert, but an interpretation and conservative conversion for practical use in fitness and diet literature.
maxFatMetabolism.notItem1: • A direct measurement from the Alpert study (it is a practical interpretation)
maxFatMetabolism.notItem2: • A guaranteed rate of fat loss
maxFatMetabolism.isText: A practical tool for estimating a safe calorie deficit based on scientific research and practical experience.
maxFatMetabolism.sourcesScientificText: Alpert SS. A limit on the energy transfer rate from the human fat store in hypophagia. Journal of Theoretical Biology. 2005 Mar 7;233(1):1–13. doi: 10.1016/j.jtbi.2004.09.015. Epub 2004 Dec 8. PMID: 15615615.
maxFatMetabolism.sourcesPracticalText: Lyle McDonald (The Rapid Fat Loss Handbook), Tom Venuto (Burn the Fat Blog)

normalizedFFMI.section1Intro: is an adjustment of the standard FFMI value to compensate for differences in body height. Taller people naturally have higher FFMI values, so normalization makes it possible to compare muscle mass more fairly between people of different heights.
normalizedFFMI.section2Note: The formula normalizes all values to a reference height of 1.8 meters (180 cm).
normalizedFFMI.section3Item1Text: naturally have higher FFMI values due to increased muscle mass relative to body height
normalizedFFMI.section4Item1: Compare muscle mass independent of body height
normalizedFFMI.section4Item2: Assess whether muscle mass is within natural limits
normalizedFFMI.section4Item3: Set realistic goals for muscle building
normalizedFFMI.section5Text: Just like FFMI, normalized FFMI is affected by the accuracy of the body fat measurement. An inaccurate measurement gives inaccurate values.

tdee.section1Intro: is your total daily energy requirement — the sum of all energy the body expends during a day.
tdee.section1Outro: TDEE is the central value for weight management and is used to plan energy intake depending on goals (weight loss, weight maintenance, or weight gain).
tdee.section2Intro: TDEE consists of several different components:
tdee.comp1Text: — energy expenditure at rest. Typically around 60% of TDEE in adults with low physical activity, but the proportion decreases with high physical activity.
tdee.comp2Text: — daily movement that is not exercise (walking, standing, cleaning). Can account for approximately 15–30% of TDEE, but the variation depends on lifestyle and occupation.
tdee.comp3Text: — energy from planned exercise. Low (<5%) in sedentary individuals but can account for 15–30% or more in elite athletes.
tdee.comp4Text: — energy required to digest and store food. Relatively constant (8–15%), but affected by dietary composition.
tdee.maintainText: Eat approximately as much as your TDEE. This is the definition of energy balance: energy in = energy out.
tdee.maintainNote: Small daily variations are normal, but over time intake should match TDEE for weight stability.
tdee.cuttingText: Eat less than your TDEE. Recommended deficit: 10–30%.
tdee.cuttingItem3: 25–30%: Aggressive — fast weight loss, short-term only
tdee.bulkingText: Eat more than your TDEE. Recommended surplus: 10–20%.
tdee.tip1Text: — start with the calculated value and adjust based on results
tdee.tip2Text: — track your calorie intake for at least 2–4 weeks before adjusting
tdee.tip3Text: — focus on trends over weeks, not daily variations
tdee.tip4Text: — if you see no results after 3–4 weeks, adjust calorie intake by 100–200 kcal

palVsMet.section1Intro: are both used to estimate energy expenditure — but in different ways.
palVsMet.section2Intro: is a multiplier that describes your total energy expenditure relative to your BMR/RMR over a full day.
palVsMet.section2Note: PAL values account for all activity during a 24-hour period.
palVsMet.section3Intro: is a measure of energy expenditure for a specific activity at a specific time.
palVsMet.metItem1: ✓ Specific activity at a point in time
palVsMet.metItem2: ✓ Used to calculate calories for a single activity
palVsMet.section5Intro: The relationship between steps per day and a reasonable estimate of PAL.
palVsMet.tableRow3Pal: ~1.6+ depending on pace and other activity
palVsMet.section7Intro: MET describes the intensity of an activity. How MET should be used depends on whether your PAL value already includes exercise or only reflects daily activity.
palVsMet.palIncludesNote: If the PAL value includes both daily activity and exercise, no additional calculation is needed. The energy expenditure from exercise is already included in PAL.
palVsMet.palExcludesTitle: PAL is based on daily life (no exercise)
palVsMet.palExcludesNote: Here PAL represents only daily activity (NEAT), and exercise is added separately. MET − 1 is used because only the extra energy above rest should be added — resting expenditure is already included in RMR × PAL.
palVsMet.summaryItem1: Use PAL alone if it includes exercise
palVsMet.summaryItem2: Use PAL + (MET − 1) if exercise is added separately
palVsMet.summaryItem3: Never use full MET together with RMR × PAL, as this leads to double-counting of resting expenditure
